judgments.comparative_verdict: Comparable — both reports hit all four expectations with correct root causes and concrete fixes; Report 2 is marginally crisper on prescribing fail-closed for Redis, while Report 1 adds more depth (missing TTL, batch-abort), so they are effectively equivalent.

report_1.evidence: Because the handler is a single bare `except Exception`, **all six are handled identically**: log a warning via `log_fallover` and retry the *same payload* against `secondary`... `BadRequestError` ... **Not appropriate** — should surface, not fail over... `ContentPolicyError` ... **Not appropriate** — should surface and stop... `AuthError` ... **Not appropriate** — should surface. ... fail over only on transient classes and re-raise the rest

report_2.evidence: It catches **bare `Exception`**, so *every* error class the primary can raise is handled identically: log a warning and re-send the same payload to the secondary vendor... `BadRequestError` — definitively non-retryable; failing over is **wrong**... `AuthError` — non-retryable... **wrong**... `ContentPolicyError` — the primary vendor *refused the content*; failing over is **the most dangerous case**... **Fix:** replace the bare catch with an explicit non-retryable denylist and only fail over the rest

report_1.evidence: Path A — non-production bypass: `if os.environ.get("STAGE") != "production": return True`... the safe direction (limit on) should not depend on an exact opt-in string. ... Path D — redis failure: `redis.incr` raises `RedisError` → `return True` (fail-open). ... during a redis outage all traffic passes unthrottled

report_2.evidence: Path A — `STAGE` != `"production"` → returns `True`... gates on an "is this production" name check — precisely what Guardrail 3 prohibits... **Fix:** fail closed by default... Path D — `incr` raises `RedisError` → returns `True` (allow). This **fails open when the backing store is down**... **Fix:** `except RedisError: return False`

report_1.evidence: `input_text` — the **full raw intake-form body**... This is the sensitive personal data itself, dumped verbatim into logs... `metadata` — logged verbatim with no schema/whitelist... Concrete fix: never log `input_text` (drop it, or log only a non-reversible digest...); ... restrict `metadata` to an explicit allowlist of non-PII fields

report_2.evidence: `record.input_text` — the **entire intake document verbatim**. This is the sensitive free-text personal data itself, dumped into logs uncontrolled... `record.metadata` — the **whole dict passed through with no filtering**... **Fix:** ... never log `input_text` verbatim — drop it or store only a hash/length/truncation, and replace the metadata pass-through with an explicit allowlist of safe keys

report_1.evidence: if `PII_SALT` is unset (or empty), the salt silently defaults to `""`, so the output is an **unsalted** `sha256(applicant_id)` with **no error or warning**... Concrete fix: fail fast if the salt is missing/empty (`raise RuntimeError("PII_SALT required")`)

report_2.evidence: when `PII_SALT` is unset it **silently falls back to an empty string**, producing an **unsalted SHA-256 of the raw applicant id**... a missing required secret (a salt) must make the dependent path fail hard, never silently fall back to an unsalted path. **Fix:** fail closed
